title: Fifty: The Under-Rated Decade
subtitle: A reading on what the research says about life satisfaction in the 50s
excerpt: A reading on the pattern that shows up consistently in life satisfaction research: that wellbeing dips in the mid-40s and then rises through the 50s into the 60s. What this means and why the decade tends to be better than it is expected to be.
sourceNotes: Draws on published happiness economics and life satisfaction research, including Blanchflower and Oswald's work on the U-shape of wellbeing across the life course. Framing is original to homemade.education.

content.text: The data on life satisfaction across the life course consistently shows a U-shape. Wellbeing is relatively high in young adulthood, reaches a trough in the mid-40s to early 50s, and then rises again through the late 50s and into the 60s. This is not an optimistic interpretation of uncertain data. It is one of the more replicated findings in life satisfaction research, observed across countries and income levels.

The mid-life dip corresponds with the period of maximum competing demands: career at its most intense, children still at home, parents often beginning to need support. It also corresponds with a reckoning between the life that was expected and the life that actually arrived. The gap between the two is felt most acutely around 45 to 50, when there is still enough time ahead to matter and enough distance behind to take stock.

Several things shift in the 50s that the research associates with improved wellbeing. Social comparison reduces: people in their 50s report spending less mental energy measuring themselves against others. Priorities clarify: the things that matter are more clearly distinguished from the things that seemed to matter. Tolerance for situations and relationships that are not working tends to decrease, which means a higher proportion of time spent on the things that are.

The 50s also bring other shifts for many people. Children grow up and need less daily care. Careers reach a steady point that leaves room to pick and choose. Awareness of mortality grows too, which tends to make the present feel more worth holding on to.
What the cultural story gets wrong

The cultural narrative around 50 is primarily one of loss: lost youth, lost options, declining relevance. The research picture is different. The decade is consistently rated more positively by people who have lived through it than by people who are approaching it. The anticipation of it is typically worse than the reality. This is not universal (external circumstances vary significantly) but as a pattern it is robust enough to be worth knowing.
